Summary: In a closed-door hearing about the legality of two grand jury subpoenas, G.A. Massuco-LaTaif, the Chief of the Criminal Division of the US Attorney's Office in DC, acknowledged that the Justice Department did not yet have evidence of wrongdoing in its criminal investigation of the Federal Reserve over the cost of its building renovations. Judge Boasberg, who presided over the hearing, found the subpoenas to be an illegal effort by the Trump administration to pressure Fed Chair Jerome Powell.

Summary: US District Judge Patrick Schiltz rejected six Justice Department grand jury subpoenas against Minnesota Governor Tim Walz and five other Minnesota Democrats. Schiltz wrote that the indictments were an effort to harass or coerce political opponents and therefore unlawful. He also added that it is unconstitutional for the federal government to force states to enforce federal laws.

Citation (MLA): Montague, Zach. "Federal Citizenship Data Tool Cannot Be Used to Screen Voters, Judge Rules." The New York Times, 22 June 2026. https://www.nytimes.com/2026/06/22/us/politics/federal-citizenship-database-voting.html
Violation/Relevance: Judge Sooknanan found that the administration's attempt to let the Trump administration create a database for checking voter eligibility was unlawful.
Summary: Judge Sparkle L. Sooknanan found it unlawful for the Trump administration to let states use a centralized database of citizens for checking immigration status to screen their voter rolls. Judge Sooknanan found that it was unlawful for the government to use sensitive data stored by the government for other purposes in the database. Sooknanan also wrote that it was unlawful for the government to use the database while it admits that the data may be flawed and may disenfranchise eligible voters.

Violation/Relevance: The 2nd Circuit Court of Appeals found ICE's mandatory detention policy unlawful, while two other circuit courts upheld the law. The matter may be resolved by the Supreme Court.
Summary: In a 3-0 ruling, the 2nd Circuit Court of Appeals found that ICE's policy mandating detention for most immigrants facing deportation is not authorized by the law and raises constitutional concerns. The policy had been upheld by the 5th Circuit Court and the 8th Circuit Court.

Summary: Federal District Judge Anthony Trenga blocked the FBI from searching through devices belonging to Washington Post reporter Hannah Natanson, upholding a ruling by a magistrate judge, finding that the prolonged seizure would affect Natanson's ability to do her job and may have harassing and chilling effects on the reporter, whether intentional or not. Furthermore, Judge Trenga found that the 1980 Privacy Protection Act protected much of the seized materials.

Violation/Relevance: Judge Talwani found that President Trump's Executive Order on mail-in voting violated the US Constitution.
Summary: Federal Judge Indira Talwani struck down parts of President Trump's Executive Order on mail-in voting. The ruling invalidated two provisions: an effort for the DHS to create state-by-state citizenship lists, and a directive for the Postal Service to use an approved list of voters to guide election mail. Judge Tawani found that the Constitution reserves the power to determine voter eligibility to the states alone.

Source: Justices Expand Trump’s Power to Fire Officials - NYT
Citation (MLA): Marimow, Ann. "Justices Expand Trump’s Power to Fire Officials." The New York Times, 29 June 2026. https://www.nytimes.com/2026/06/29/us/politics/supreme-court-independent-regulator-firings.html
Violation/Relevance: The decision overturns Humphrey's Executor and expands Presidential control over independent agencies like the FTC. Humphrey's Executor, decided in 1935, approved the creation of independent agencies, insulating some appointees from Presidential authority.
Summary: In a 6-3 decision, the Supreme Court ruled that President Trump could fire FTC Board Member Rebecca Kelly Slaughter without cause. The case overturned Humphrey's Executor, with Chief Justice John Roberts writing that “neither Congress nor the courts may saddle” the president with executive branch officials “with whom he cannot work.” The decision fell along partisan lines, with the 3 democratic appointees dissenting. Robert's majority opinion carved out an exception for the Federal Reserve.

Source: Supreme Court Blocks Trump From Firing Fed Governor Lisa Cook - WSJ
Citation (MLA): Rosomer, James, and Nick Timiraos. "Supreme Court Blocks Trump From Firing Fed Governor Lisa Cook." The Wall Street Journal, 29 June 2026. https://www.wsj.com/us-news/law/supreme-court-blocks-trumps-fed-firing-but-allows-removals-at-other-agencies-350c8cf4?mod=law_news_article_pos2
Violation/Relevance: The ruling appears to reaffirm the independence of the Federal Reserve and requires judicial accountability for cause removals. It finds that President Trump's effort to fire Lisa Cook without meaningful scrutiny violates the spirit of the law that created the Federal Reserve.
Summary: In a 5-4 ruling, the Supreme Court blocked President Trump from firing Lisa Cook on procedural grounds. The ruling required procedural protections that allowed evidence to be aired in order to fire a Fed chair, in order to maintain the fact and appearance of independence. The ruling keeps Cook in office, but allows the President's effort to proceed with judicial checks. Chief Justice John Roberts wrote the majority opinion, joined by Justices Kavanaugh, Sotomayor, Kagan, and Brown Jackson.

Source: Supreme Court Upholds Mississippi Late-Arriving Mail-In Ballot Law - NYT
Citation (MLA): VanSickle, Abbie. "Supreme Court Upholds Mississippi Late-Arriving Mail-In Ballot Law." The New York Times, 29 June 2026. https://www.nytimes.com/2026/06/29/us/politics/supreme-court-late-mail-ballots.html
Violation/Relevance: The ruling upholds state laws on mail-in voting and rejects a push by the Trump administration to limit the practice. The decision carries significant on state authority over election laws and practices.
Summary: In a 5-4 decision, the Supreme Court upheld a Mississippi law that allowed election officials to count ballots postmarked by election day and received up to five business days later. The majority, in an opinion written by Justice Barrett, found that nothing in federal statutes required ballots to be received by election day. Barrett was joined by Chief Justice Roberts and Justices Sotomayor, Kagan, and Brown Jackson.
